title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Life of Ma Parker
content.subtitle: An extract from the short story by Katherine Mansfield.
content.text: When the literary gentleman, whose flat old Ma Parker cleaned every Tuesday, opened the door to her that morning, he looked at her for a moment and said: 'Oh, is that you, Ma Parker?' 'Yes, sir,' she answered. 'I hope you haven't been waiting long?' 'No, sir, not long.' 'I'm glad. Come in.' [31] 'We buried him yesterday, sir,' she said. 'Who?' asked the gentleman. 'My little grandson.' [/31] || [32] The literary gentleman had a system of his own. He didn't want any 'housework' done. He just wanted someone to come in and 'tidy up'. He was always wondering why people made such a fuss about housework. It was so simple. [/32] He believed that if you just put things back where they belonged, there was no need for all the cleaning. || Ma Parker didn't say anything to contradict him. [33] She pitied the poor gentleman. [/33] To her, he seemed helpless in the face of life's practicalities. He didn't even seem to know how to keep a kitchen table clean or manage the basic chores without her constant intervention. || Ma Parker was a person who kept her thoughts to herself, though her life was written in the lines of her face. [34] The neighbours, when they saw her coming home at night after a long day's work, used to say, 'She's had a hard life, has Ma Parker.' [/34] And she had. From her early days as a kitchen maid to her later years, she had known every kind of trouble and sorrow. || Her mind often drifted back to her childhood, though those days felt like they belonged to another person entirely. [35] The memories of Stratford-on-Avon were few and scattered. [/35] She could recall the smell of the damp earth and the cold of the morning, but the details of her home there were becoming increasingly difficult to hold onto. || When she had first arrived in London as a young woman, she had been horrified by the conditions of the city. She had been terrified of the 'black beetles'—the cockroaches—that infested the kitchens where she worked. But after decades of labor, she had grown indifferent. [36] It was as if you said you'd never seen your own feet. [/36] They were simply an inevitable and unremarkable part of the environment in which she spent her life.

questions.question: What do we learn about Ma Parker in the first paragraph?
options: A) She doesn't like cleaning the man's house.
B) She is about to go to a funeral.
C) There has been a death in her family.
D) She works for a man who is ill.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly mentions that they buried her little grandson the day before.

questions.question: What does the author suggest about the literary gentleman?
options: A) He never did anything for himself.
B) He didn't think housework was a problem.
C) He thought he was too important to do housework.
D) He always had enough clean forks.
questions.explanation: The author notes that he had a 'system' and wondered why people made a 'fuss' about housework, as he thought it was 'simple'.

questions.question: Regarding the gentleman, Ma Parker...
options: A) felt sorry for the man.
B) was shocked at how untidy the kitchen was.
C) was sad whenever she entered the kitchen.
D) wished the man had a wife.
questions.explanation: The text states: 'She pitied the poor gentleman', showing she felt sympathy for his helplessness.

questions.question: Ma Parker's neighbours...
options: A) annoyed her.
B) were worried about her.
C) understood what her life was like.
D) were proud of her.
questions.explanation: The neighbours' comment that she's had a 'hard life' indicates they were aware of and understood her difficult circumstances.

questions.question: The author mentions Stratford-on-Avon to show that...
options: A) Ma Parker came from a theatrical background.
B) life there was completely different to life in London.
C) Ma Parker didn't have pleasant memories of living there.
D) Ma Parker's memories of the place were fading.
questions.explanation: The text describes her memories as 'few and scattered', implying they are disappearing over time.

questions.question: When Ma Parker thinks 'It was as if you said you'd never seen your own feet', she means...
options: A) she thought the beetles were funny.
B) the beetles made cleaning the house unpleasant.
C) it was common to see beetles in London.
D) beetles were always getting under her feet.
questions.explanation: The comparison suggests that seeing the beetles was as common and expected as seeing a part of her own body.
